title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about a jazz musician. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Language of Silence
content.subtitle: How one musician found her voice by saying less
content.text:
Clara Mendez was already thirty-four when she first sat at a concert grand piano with the intention of performing for an audience. Most of her peers in the jazz world had been playing since childhood, their fingers moulded to the keys almost before they could read. [31]Yet Clara had come to music through an unusual route: a decade working as a sound engineer had given her an understanding of music that was architectural rather than instinctive, built from the outside in rather than the inside out.[/31]

Her debut at the Blue Note in New York was met with cautious curiosity. Critics noted an unusual quality in her playing — a kind of deliberate restraint that was at odds with the virtuoso displays typical of the jazz scene at the time. Where other pianists filled every available space with notes, Clara left gaps. [32]Some found this maddening. One reviewer called her performance 'an extended exercise in withholding.' Another described it as 'the most honest set of the decade.' The debate her playing generated was itself a sign that something significant was happening.[/32]

Clara herself seemed indifferent to the division her music created. In interviews, she rarely spoke about technical matters, preferring to discuss the emotional architecture of a piece. [33]'I am not interested in impressing anyone,' she told one journalist. 'I am interested in the moment when a listener forgets to listen — when the music becomes something they are simply inside.'[/33] This philosophy struck many as either arrogance or profound self-knowledge.

Her second album, Intervals, sold poorly on release but found a devoted following over the following years. [34]The album was recorded in a decommissioned factory on the outskirts of Chicago, a choice Clara made not for aesthetic reasons but because the natural acoustics of the space transformed the piano's resonance in ways she could not have planned.[/34] The recordings carry a sense of space and distance that studio environments rarely capture.

What sets Clara apart from many of her contemporaries is not technical ability — by her own admission, there are dozens of pianists more gifted at the mechanics of the instrument — but the quality of her listening. [35]She has spoken at length about the practice of 'active silence,' a form of attention she developed during her years as an engineer, in which the goal is not to produce sound but to understand the space between sounds.[/35] This approach has influenced a generation of younger musicians who cite her as a primary reference.

[36]Critics who initially dismissed Clara's work have since revised their positions, many acknowledging that her restrained, contemplative style anticipated broader shifts in the jazz world toward minimalism and emotional directness.[/36] Whether she intended to be ahead of her time or simply followed her own instincts remains unclear. Clara, as always, declines to explain.

questions.question: What was unusual about Clara Mendez's path into jazz performance?
options: A) She developed her musical understanding through technical work rather than performance.
B) She had trained as a classical pianist before turning to jazz.
C) She studied music formally at a conservatoire as an adult.
D) She had worked as a music critic before becoming a performer.
questions.explanation: The text states that her decade as a sound engineer gave her an understanding of music that was 'architectural rather than instinctive, built from the outside in.'

questions.question: What does the reaction of critics to Clara's debut suggest about her music?
options: A) It was too technically demanding for most audiences to appreciate.
B) It was immediately recognised as a masterpiece by all who heard it.
C) It provoked strong and contrasting responses, indicating its impact.
D) It disappointed audiences who expected a more traditional jazz style.
questions.explanation: The sharply divided critical reaction — from 'exercise in withholding' to 'most honest set of the decade' — shows that her music had a striking and polarising effect.

questions.question: What does Clara's quote to the journalist reveal about her artistic goal?
options: A) She wants to demonstrate technical superiority over other pianists.
B) She aims to create an experience so absorbing that listeners lose self-consciousness.
C) She is primarily focused on gaining recognition in the jazz world.
D) She wants audiences to appreciate the complexity of her compositions.
questions.explanation: Clara speaks of the moment when a listener 'forgets to listen' and becomes 'simply inside' the music, suggesting she seeks total immersion rather than admiration.

questions.question: Why did Clara record her second album in a disused factory?
options: A) She wanted to save money compared with recording in a professional studio.
B) She had always admired the building's industrial history.
C) She planned to achieve a specific aesthetic effect that studios could not provide.
D) The acoustic properties of the space produced results she had not anticipated.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that her choice was 'not for aesthetic reasons' but because the acoustics 'transformed the piano's resonance in ways she could not have planned.'

questions.question: What does 'active silence' mean in the context of Clara's practice?
options: A) A focused awareness of the gaps and pauses within music.
B) A technique for reducing unwanted background noise during recording.
C) A method of composing music without using a conventional instrument.
D) A way of training students to play more quietly and with greater control.
questions.explanation: The text describes 'active silence' as a form of attention in which 'the goal is not to produce sound but to understand the space between sounds.'

questions.question: What does the final paragraph suggest about the significance of Clara's work?
options: A) She deliberately set out to change the direction of jazz music.
B) She has become the most commercially successful pianist of her generation.
C) Her approach foreshadowed wider developments in the jazz world.
D) Critics recognised the importance of her work from the very beginning.
questions.explanation: Critics acknowledge that her 'restrained, contemplative style anticipated broader shifts in the jazz world toward minimalism and emotional directness.'
